text: English) references interesting biographical information, and rightly places van Gennep’s life’s work and the oblivion to which he was exposed within the context of French academic power politics in the early twentieth century. Several of Zumwalt’s arguments will be pursued in our reconstruction here. The Italian anthropologist, Filippo Zerilli (1998a) has unearthed and contextualized a small but in some ways significant chapter of van Gennep’s life’s work, namely the major international conference he organized in Switzerland in 1914, pulling together 600 of Europe’s most important ethnographers and anthropologists, including his friend, Marcel Mauss. Zerilli’s analysis of this event (and how it related to van Gennep’s larger academic project and his vision for the social sciences as a sort of ‘biology’) will also be drawn upon. Final mention should be made of Thierry Goguel d’Allondans, who in 2002 published a book entitled, Rites de passage, rites de initation. Lecture d’Arnold van Gennep, in which Chapter 1 briefly introduces the thought of Arnold van Gennep. There exists a wealth of encyclopaedic entries on Arnold van Gennep, full of factual mistakes and contradictory biographical information. One important encyclopaedic entry was published in 1968 by Solon Kimball, the man who had Rites of Passage translated eight years earlier. It contains important elements approaching a theoretical appraisal of van Gennep’s work, and in contrast to Kimball’s earlier Introduction to the translation of Rites of Passage, he here correctly emphasizes how van Gennep’s project actually differed from the dominant French and Anglo-Saxon approaches in the social sciences. Kimball ends his entry with the following augury: Thus, van Gennep saw the tradition, methods, and goals of ethnography as different from those of both the French sociologists and the British school of anthropology. The full exposition of these differences awaits future scholarship. Liminality and the Modern 28 A recently renewed interest in the study of rites of passage is evidenced by the writings of Whiting and Child (1953), Young (1965), and Turner (1964). This resurgence may extend to further study of van Gennep’s ethnographic method and of his theoretical formulations. (Kimball 1968) Such an expectation of a van Gennep resurgence was not misplaced in 1968, and Kimball had indeed recognized the potential of van Gennep’s work. There can be no doubt that via Turner’s work, van Gennep became known to a very broad audience. However, it can be safely stated that a more general and thorough resurgence of van Gennep’s work simply did not take place in the decades that followed. And it still has not taken place. More recently, Arpad Szakolczai published a short but significant entry on van Gennep (Szakolczai 2013b). It is significant because it takes up a central argument which will be pursued throughout this book as well: that Arnold van Gennep could and should be considered an important social science ‘theoretician’, of a general and contemporary importance, exactly insofar as his theorizing went completely outside a neo-Kantian framework, greatly anticipating contemporary developments. Re-situating Arnold van Gennep: Failed Receptions The larger problem with the literature on van Gennep is easy to identify, for it follows from what happened in his life and to the reception of his academic work. Put quite simply, while alive, he was first ostracized from French sociology and anthropology, and then by and large forgotten and neglected, working in solitude most of his life, unknown to almost everyone outside French folklore – a discipline which had not even found institutional footing within that country. Details have evidently become lost. After World War I, van Gennep gave up his in-fight with French academia, so the neglect he eventually suffered may even seem justified to an extent. Van Gennep did not have any one close collaborator who could take up his work, and communicate it to a wider audience, evidencing its continued relevance – and this is also due to the fact, of course, that he had no students, due again to the simple reason that he did not have a teaching position. He apparently shared almost everything he wrote with his beloved wife. In fact, throughout their life, he used to read aloud his draft chapters to her, sitting by the fireplace in the evening. He was of the opinion that every properly written sentence must possess the right rhythm when performed orally (Ketty van Gennep 1964: 10); in itself more than a hint that his emphasis on rhythm and ritual forms was also an aesthetic recognition, linked to learning and perception. But we know very little about his wife, and she herself left no written testimony. She died three years before her husband; there was no ‘Edith Turner’ to complete the picture and facilitate a proper reception upon van Gennep’s death. An equally serious problem resides with the fact that what we do know about Arnold van Gennep, and what has indeed been discussed during the last decades, very much depends on our discipline. Folklorists know a great deal Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 29 about van Gennep, and for very good reasons they keep applying his insights and analytical distinctions, but they tend not to engage with his early and important confrontation with the Durkheimians; e.g. they this van Gennep as a folklorist – which he was also (and perhaps the biggest ever), but which was simply not the case before World War I. British and French anthropologists would re-discover van Gennep from the 1960s. However, with the significant exception of Victor Turner (whose contributions will be more fully discussed in Chapter 3), much of this appraisal was flavoured by the structuralist interpretation of Lévi-Strauss, a reception which heavily influenced people like Evans-Pritchard, Needham and Leach. The very few sociologists who have at least mentioned the importance of van Gennep (and this includes Steven Lukes) have by and large failed to take him seriously. He is mostly mentioned in the footnotes. Until recently (see Thomassen 2012d), it has not been discussed how the positions of Tarde and van Gennep with respect to the Durkheimians
